$comment: Optional. Place at .cairnci/field-governance-gate.json in your repo (or point the config-file input / CONFIG_FILE env elsewhere). The field-governance-gate inspects the PR git diff for newly created AND modified custom fields and checks that each carries the governance metadata you require. No org connection, no sf CLI — pure git + filesystem. Exit codes: 0 = ok, 10 = warn (never blocks), 1 = an error-severity finding, 2 = config/environment problem. See .github/actions/field-governance-gate/README.md.
$comment2: Attribute keys and their metadata elements: description -> <description>; inlineHelpText (aliases helpText, tooltip) -> <inlineHelpText>; businessOwner (aliases dataOwner, owner) -> <businessOwnerUser> OR <businessOwnerGroup>; businessStatus (alias fieldUsage) -> <businessStatus>; securityClassification (aliases dataSensitivity, sensitivity) -> <securityClassification>; complianceGroup (alias compliance) -> <complianceGroup>. A bare <businessOwner> element does NOT count — Salesforce rejects it at deploy.
$comment3: Policy layers, most general first: built-in defaults -> top-level severity/require -> objectTypes[<family>] -> the FIRST matching rule -> objectOverrides[<Object>]. A layer that omits severity/require inherits it; a layer that sets require replaces the whole set (use "attribute": false to drop just one).
$comment5: severity inside a require entry sets the severity for THAT TAG ALONE and beats every layer, including objectOverrides. It is the only way to say "a missing description blocks the PR but a missing tooltip only warns" — scoped rules match per field, not per tag, so every tag in one rule shares its severity. A tag that omits severity inherits the resolved layer severity, so you only name the exceptions. Usual adoption path: turn a requirement on as warn everywhere, then promote tags to error one at a time. Any key other than severity/minLength/allowed/pattern is a config error rather than silently ignored.
$comment4: Standard fields (API name not ending __c) are skipped by default: the platform accepts governance tags on them, but their metadata file is usually a partial customization rather than a field this PR authored. Set true to audit them too.

bypass.$comment: Never audited, at any layer. `objects` skips every field on those objects; `fields` takes Object.Field API names. Matching is case-insensitive and supports a trailing `*` wildcard on either segment: `Invoice__c.*` covers every field on Invoice__c, `Temp_*` covers objects with that prefix. Unions with any matching rule's own bypass.

objectTypes.$comment: Per object family: custom (__c), standard, customMetadata (__mdt), platformEvent (__e), bigObject (__b), externalObject (__x). All enabled by default. Governance metadata deploys on every family (org-verified), so these are conventions you choose, not platform limits.

rules.$comment: name is required. Rules are matched in declaration order and the FIRST match wins, so put narrow rules first and a catch-all last. Scope with `objects` and/or `fields` (same wildcard syntax as bypass); a rule with neither is the catch-all. Constraints: minLength catches placeholder text like "TBD"; allowed pins a value set (a check-only deploy does NOT reject a bogus securityClassification, so the platform will not catch typos for you); pattern takes a regex.
rules.name: pii

rules.$comment: A rule-level bypass unions with the global one above, but applies only to the fields this rule governs.
rules.name: sandbox

rules.$comment: Catch-all: no objects/fields scope, so it governs everything the rules above did not claim.
rules.name: house-default

objectOverrides.$comment: The last word — applied after any matching rule. Use it for repository-wide severity carve-outs, or with enabled:true to re-enable a single object inside a family turned off in objectTypes.
